questions.a: A database that stores self-contained documents such as JSON or BSON.

questions.q: Why do document stores help with flexible schemas?
questions.a: Different records can carry different fields without expensive schema migrations.

questions.q: When is a document store a better fit than a relational table?
questions.a: When objects are naturally nested and you usually read them as whole aggregates.
A simple mapping from key to value.

questions.q: Why are key-value stores so fast?
questions.a: They avoid complex joins and usually route directly to the stored value.

questions.q: When is a key-value store enough by itself?
questions.a: When the access pattern is mostly direct lookup by key and the value shape is simple.
A system that stores rows with flexible columns grouped by partition.

questions.q: Why are wide-column systems good for write-heavy workloads?
questions.a: They keep writes append-friendly and avoid heavy page rewrites.

questions.q: When would you choose wide-column storage?
questions.a: When your data is naturally partitioned by a key and you need high write throughput.

questions.q: What does a graph database model directly?
Why are graph databases strong at relationship queries?
questions.a: Traversals over explicit edges are cheaper than emulating the same pattern with many joins.

questions.q: When should you choose a graph database?
questions.a: When relationships and multi-hop traversals are central to the workload.

questions.q: What is special about time-series or search-oriented NoSQL systems?
questions.a: They optimize for timestamped writes or indexed text queries.

questions.q: Why do time-series systems use retention or downsampling?
questions.a: They keep recent high-resolution data while reducing storage for older observations.

questions.q: When would a time-series or search engine beat a general-purpose database?
questions.a: When the query pattern is dominated by time windows, aggregations, or full-text search.

questions.q: What do sharding and replica sets solve?
questions.a: Sharding spreads data across nodes; replica sets improve availability.

questions.q: Why is partition-key choice so important in NoSQL systems?
questions.a: It determines how evenly data and traffic are distributed across the cluster.

questions.q: How does CAP or PACELC shape NoSQL design?
questions.a: You must trade consistency, availability, latency, and partition tolerance against each other.
